questions.question: Why are long-lived static secrets risky in large systems?
options: They increase compromise blast radius because leaked credentials remain useful for too long.
They prevent workloads from using TLS.
They only work for databases and not APIs.
They make audit logging impossible.
questions.explanation: Long-lived credentials are harder to rotate and remain exploitable for longer after leakage.

questions.question: What is the main value of credential rotation?
options: It changes hostnames so clients discover services faster.
It reduces exposure from stale or leaked material and proves the process works before emergencies.
It removes the need for workload identity.
It guarantees zero downtime automatically.
questions.explanation: Rotation both reduces risk and tests operational readiness before a real compromise.

questions.question: Why should most production systems avoid fetching secrets on the request hot path?
options: Because secret managers cannot authenticate workloads.
Because it turns every request into a dependency on secret-store latency and availability.
Because secrets can only be used at process startup.
Because caches are incompatible with credentials.
questions.explanation: Hot-path secret fetches create central latency and outage amplification.

questions.question: What is the purpose of an overlap window during rotation?
options: It allows old and new credentials or trust bundles to coexist safely during staged rollout.
It doubles credential lifetime permanently for reliability.
It removes the need for revocation.
It guarantees every node refreshes at the same millisecond.
questions.explanation: Overlap windows prevent cliff-edge cutovers during cert or key updates.

questions.question: Which metric best detects that cert rotation is harming production traffic?
options: Handshake or authentication failure rate after rollout.
Average HTML page length.
Only CPU idle time.
Count of README files.
questions.explanation: Handshake failures reveal trust-chain or credential mismatch immediately.

questions.question: What trade-off comes with very short credential TTLs?
options: Smaller compromise window but more refresh traffic and tighter dependence on refresh automation.
Perfect revocation with no operational overhead.
Lower audit needs because secrets expire quickly.
Guaranteed better cache hit ratio.
questions.explanation: Short TTLs improve containment but increase renewal pressure.

questions.question: What is the strongest first response when a workload credential is believed to be leaked?
options: Wait for the next normal deployment cycle.
Revoke the affected version, re-issue scoped credentials, and watch for continued use.
Delete all audit logs to reduce noise.
Disable TLS across the fleet temporarily.
questions.explanation: Containment requires revocation, re-issuance, and monitoring for residual use.

questions.question: Why is workload identity better than sharing one secret across many services?
options: It improves attribution and allows narrower, service-specific credentials.
It guarantees no secret rotation is needed.
It removes the need for mTLS.
It works only in single-region systems.
questions.explanation: Per-workload identity improves scope control, auditing, and blast-radius reduction.
